title: Pseudoacromegaly Syndromes
authors: Marta Korbonits, MD, PhD; Pedro Marques, MD, PhD

items.title: Why pseudoacromegaly must be considered in acromegaloid patients
items.text: Several rare genetic and metabolic conditions mimic acromegaloid facies and acral enlargement without GH/IGF-1 excess; awareness prevents years of misdirected pituitary workup.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Awareness of these rare conditions will help the differential diagnosis in patients with acromegaloid features but no GH excess."

items.title: How to approach the differential diagnosis of pseudoacromegaly
items.text: Organize features into acromegaloid habitus (tall, normal, or short), primarily tall stature, or biochemical GH/IGF-1 discordance; gestalt, dysmorphism, pubertal timing, and intellectual disability narrow the list.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "the main features can be assigned to 3 categories: (1) primarily acromegaloid features with tall, normal, or short stature; (2) primarily tall stature; and (3) biochemical abnormalities suggestive of high GH or IGF-1 levels."

items.title: How urinary PGE2 helps diagnose pachydermoperiostosis
items.text: Clubbing, forehead furrowing, young-onset arthralgia, and lean habitus suggest pachydermoperiostosis from SLCO2A1 or HPGD variants; elevated urinary PGE2 with low metabolites points to HPGD deficiency.
items.reference: Case 1: "In patients with HPGD pathogenic variants, there is elevated urinary PGE2 (Answer D), with low/undetectable PGE2 metabolites"

items.title: Cantú syndrome: hypertrichosis and cardiac findings
items.text: Infant-onset generalized hypertrichosis with coarse facies, cardiomegaly, PDA, and pericardial effusions suggests Cantú syndrome from activating ABCC9 or KCNJ8 variants—minoxidil can phenocopy via K-ATP channel activation.
items.reference: Case 2: "Hypertrichotic osteochondrodysplasia is caused by activating pathogenic variants in the ABCC9 gene (sulfonylurea receptor 2) or, rarely, its partner the KCNJ8 gene"

items.title: Why oral glucose suppression testing is challenging in adolescence
items.text: GH nadir after OGTT is pubertal-stage and gender specific—highest in Tanner 2–3 girls (1.57 ng/mL)—and IGF-1 correlates better with pubertal stage than chronological age.
items.reference: Biochemical Alterations: "GH nadir is highest in Tanner stage 2 to 3 girls (1.57 ng/mL [1.57 μg/L]) and lower in other pubetal stages (0.64 ng/mL [0.64 μg/L]) or for boys (0.5 ng/mL [0.5 μg/L])."

items.title: Why IGSF1 deficiency elevates GH and IGF-1 without acromegaly
items.text: IGSF1 deficiency is the only known condition with elevated GH and IGF-1 without clinical acromegaly—central hypothyroidism and testicular enlargement are part of the syndrome.
items.reference: Biochemical Alterations: "There is only one known disease condition in which both high GH and IGF-1 can be seen without the patient having clinical acromegaly: the recently described IGSF1 deficiency syndrome."

items.title: How X-linked acrogigantism presents in early childhood
items.text: GPR101 duplication causes infant-onset GH/IGF-1 and prolactin excess with pituitary hyperplasia—almost 80% of patients are female; normal birth weight with rapid growth under age 1 is typical.
items.reference: Case 3: "Almost 80% of patients with X-linked acrogigantism are female."

items.title: Sotos, Weaver, and related overgrowth syndromes
items.text: Rapid childhood growth with acromegaloid features, typical gestalt, and intellectual disability characterize several overgrowth syndromes—clinical phenotyping precedes targeted gene panels.
items.reference: Acromegaloid Features: "Acromegaloid features with rapid growth in childhood are typical of several overgrowth syndromes such as Sotos syndrome, Weaver syndrome, Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome, and Tatton-Brown syndrome"

items.title: How minoxidil mimics Cantú syndrome
items.text: Prolonged minoxidil activates ATP-sensitive potassium channels like Cantú syndrome, producing acromegaloid skin changes and hypertrichosis.
items.reference: Acromegaloid Features: "Longstanding minoxidil treatment, via its stimulating activity on the ATP potassium channel, produces symptoms similar to those observed in Cantú syndrome, acromegaloid skin changes, and hypertrichosis."

items.title: Pitfall: decades-long misdiagnosis of pseudoacromegaly
items.text: Rare conditions are often missed—reported cases were referred to endocrinology four times over 40 years before correct genetic diagnosis.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "We have reported a case in which a patient was referred to endocrinology on 4 different occasions over 40 years, from age 10 to 50 years, by the time the correct diagnosis was made."

items.title: Miura-type epiphyseal chondrodysplasia and toe length
items.text: Extreme tall stature in young children with extra-long halluces can indicate natriuretic peptide C pathway disorders such as Miura-type epiphyseal chondrodysplasia.
items.reference: Case 3: "Extra-long halluces could be diagnostic for diseases affecting the natriuretic peptide C pathway."

items.title: When to refer pseudoacromegaly patients for genetics
items.text: After excluding endocrine GH excess, most pseudoacromegaly conditions are genetic—genetic consultation is recommended.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Most pseudoacromegaly conditions have a genetic origin, so referral for genetic consultation is recommended if endocrine causes have been ruled out."

items.question: A 26-year-old man has tall stature, clubbing, forehead furrowing, sweating since age 15, and lean habitus. Best diagnostic step?
items.explanation: Clubbing with pachydermia suggests pachydermoperiostosis; urinary PGE2 helps distinguish HPGD from SLCO2A1 subtypes.
items.reference: Case 1: "Measuring baseline pituitary hormones (Answer B) can help rule out acromegaly."

items.question: A woman has coarse facies, thickened fingers, and infant-onset generalized hypertrichosis in multiple family members with pericardial effusions. Best approach?
options: Type clinical features into a search engine to identify syndromes
Immediate pituitary MRI without biochemical testing
items.explanation: Cantú syndrome has a broad multisystem phenotype—pattern recognition via clinical search can accelerate diagnosis before genetic confirmation.
items.reference: Case 2: "Typing clinical features into Google (Answer C) may be helpful."

items.question: A 4-year-old girl has height +5 SD without family tall stature. Which diagnosis should be included in the differential?
items.explanation: Extreme childhood tall stature with long toes suggests Miura-type epiphyseal chondrodysplasia; XLAG causes infant-onset acrogigantism.
items.reference: Case 3: "Answer: H) Epiphyseal chondrodysplasia, Miura type"

items.question: Pachydermoperiostosis is caused by pathogenic variants in:
items.explanation: SLCO2A1 (PGE2 transporter) and HPGD (15-PGDH) defects raise PGE2 and produce clubbing and pachydermia.
items.reference: Case 1: "biallelic pathogenic variants in the transporter SLCO2A1 gene where PGE2 is degraded, or due to defective enzymatic clearing of PGE2 caused by biallelic pathogenic variants in the HPGD gene"

items.question: Cantú syndrome is caused by activating variants in:
items.explanation: ABCC9 (SUR2) and rarely KCNJ8 (Kir6.1) encode ATP-sensitive K+ channel subunits.
items.reference: Case 2: "activating pathogenic variants in the ABCC9 gene (sulfonylurea receptor 2) or, rarely, its partner the KCNJ8 gene"

items.question: Physiological states with both elevated GH and IGF-1 include:
items.explanation: Pregnancy and adolescence are the two physiological states with elevated GH and IGF-1.
items.reference: Table. Conditions Associated With Discrepant GH and/or IGF-1 Levels: "Adolescence"

items.question: X-linked acrogigantism is due to:
items.explanation: GPR101 duplication on X chromosome causes infant-onset pituitary hyperplasia/tumor with GH, IGF-1, and usually prolactin excess.
items.reference: Case 3: "X-linked acrogigantism is due to duplication of the orphan receptor GPR101 gene"

items.question: In evaluating tall stature, Marfan syndrome is suggested by:
options: Intellectual disability as a universal feature
Ectopia lentis in nearly 60% of cases
items.explanation: Ectopia lentis is a hallmark; cardiovascular aortic dilation and mitral prolapse are evaluated with Ghent criteria.
items.reference: Case 3: "ectopia lentis is the hallmark feature seen in nearly 60% of cases."

items.question: Which medication can produce a phenocopy of Cantú syndrome?
items.explanation: Minoxidil activates the same K-ATP channels, causing hypertrichosis and acromegaloid skin changes.
items.reference: Acromegaloid Features: "Longstanding minoxidil treatment, via its stimulating activity on the ATP potassium channel, produces symptoms similar to those observed in Cantú syndrome"

items.question: IGSF1 deficiency syndrome is characterized by:
options: Low GH and IGF-1 with gigantism
High GH and IGF-1 without clinical acromegaly
Normal GH axis with acromegaloid facies only
items.explanation: IGSF1 loss-of-function is the unique high GH/IGF-1 without acromegaly syndrome.
items.reference: Biochemical Alterations: "both high GH and IGF-1 can be seen without the patient having clinical acromegaly: the recently described IGSF1 deficiency syndrome."

items.question: Which feature favors an overgrowth syndrome over pituitary gigantism in a tall child?
options: Acromegaloid habitus with increased appetite only
GH nadir <0.4 ng/mL on OGTT in a pubertal boy
items.explanation: Overgrowth syndromes often show gestalt and developmental delay; pituitary gigantism shows true GH/IGF-1 excess.
items.reference: Case 3: "Is there evidence of intellectual disability or central nervous system abnormalities? This would suggest overgrowth syndromes"

items.question: Clubbing in pachydermoperiostosis shares mechanism with lung disease because:
options: The lung is the primary site for PGE2 degradation
Both are treated with somatostatin analogues
items.explanation: SLCO2A1 defects impair pulmonary PGE2 uptake/degradation—same pathway implicated in pulmonary clubbing.
items.reference: Case 1: "the mechanism is probably linked, as lung is the primary site for PGE2 degradation."

items.question: In any patient with acromegaloid features, the essential first endocrine step is:
options: Urinary prostaglandin E2 before GH testing
Measurement of GH and IGF-1 to exclude true acromegaly
items.explanation: True acromegaly must be excluded biochemically before pursuing rare pseudoacromegaly diagnoses.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "which should lead to the prompt assessment of the GH/IGF-1 axis."

items.statement: Pseudoacromegaly conditions can include acromegaloid facies, prognathism, visceromegaly, and hyperhidrosis without GH excess.
items.explanation: Overlapping features are listed in the significance section.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Overlapping features can include acromegaloid facies and acral enlargement, prognathism, visceromegaly, hypertension, fatigue, headaches, arthralgias, paresthesia, hyperhidrosis"

items.statement: Cantú syndrome commonly presents with suppressed gonadotropins and primary amenorrhea.
items.explanation: Cantú features hypertrichosis, coarse facies, cardiac enlargement, and joint hyperextensibility—not primary hypogonadism.
items.reference: Case 2: "The most prominent manifestation is the generalized hypertrichosis, in addition to the coarse facial features, bulbous nose, thick lips"

items.statement: IGF-1 peaks around the 37th week of pregnancy at 2–3 times nonpregnant levels.
items.explanation: Pregnancy creates a unique GH/IGF axis with placental GH and protease effects.
items.reference: Biochemical Alterations: "increased IGF-1 peaking at the 37th week of pregnancy."

items.statement: Prolonged phenytoin use can induce some acromegaloid features.
items.explanation: Drug adverse effects are listed among pseudoacromegaly causes.
items.reference: Acromegaloid Features: "Phenytoin can induce some acromegaloid features."

items.explanation: Almost 80% of XLAG patients are female.
items.reference: Case 3: "Almost 80% of patients with X-linked acrogigantism are female."

items.statement: In HPGD-related pachydermoperiostosis, urinary PGE2 is elevated with low or undetectable PGE2 metabolites.
items.explanation: This biochemical pattern distinguishes HPGD from SLCO2A1 subtypes.
items.reference: Case 1: "there is elevated urinary PGE2 (Answer D), with low/undetectable PGE2 metabolites"

items.statement: Distinctive forehead appearance and gestalt characterize Sotos syndrome among acromegaloid differentials.
items.explanation: Key facial gestalt aids differentiation.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Key facial features (the overall "gestalt") may characterize Sotos syndrome."

items.statement: Increased tissue responsiveness to GH (e.g., GH receptor D3 genotype) can cause normal GH with high IGF-1.
items.explanation: Listed under normal GH and high IGF-1 conditions.
items.reference: Table. Conditions Associated With Discrepant GH and/or IGF-1 Levels: "Increased tissue responsiveness to GH (eg, genotype D3 of the GH receptor)"

items.assertion: Assertion: Patients with pseudoacromegaly may be referred repeatedly before correct diagnosis.
items.reason: Reason: Most physicians lack experience with these rare usually genetic disorders.
items.explanation: Both true—awareness barrier causes diagnostic delay.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "Because pseudoacromegaly conditions are rare, most physicians have no experience with these diseases, thus hindering diagnosis."

items.assertion: Assertion: Clubbing in a young patient with acromegaloid features suggests pachydermoperiostosis.
items.reason: Reason: Pachydermoperiostosis is caused by GH hypersecretion from a pituitary adenoma.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—mechanism is PGE2 metabolism, not GH excess.
items.reference: Case 1: "The key clinical diagnostic clue in this case is the clubbing"

items.assertion: Assertion: Minoxidil can phenocopy Cantú syndrome.
items.reason: Reason: Minoxidil activates ATP-sensitive potassium channels like activating ABCC9 variants.
items.explanation: Both true and mechanistically linked.
items.reference: Case 2: "minoxidil activates this potassium channel and is of course used to induce hair growth."

items.assertion: Assertion: GH may fail to suppress adequately on OGTT during pubertal growth spurts.
items.reason: Reason: GH nadir thresholds are identical for all pubertal stages and genders.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—nadir is stage and gender specific.
items.reference: Biochemical Alterations: "GH levels are increased, particularly at the peak of pubertal growth, and often do not fully suppress during an oral glucose tolerance test."

items.assertion: Assertion: Genetic consultation is recommended when endocrine causes of acromegaly are excluded.
items.reason: Reason: Most pseudoacromegaly conditions have a genetic origin.
items.explanation: Both true per main conclusions.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Most pseudoacromegaly conditions have a genetic origin, so referral for genetic consultation is recommended if endocrine causes have been ruled out."

items.assertion: Assertion: X-linked acrogigantism should be considered in extreme childhood tall stature.
items.reason: Reason: XLAG is due to GPR101 duplication causing GH/IGF-1 and usually prolactin excess.
items.explanation: Both true—only endocrine cause of such extreme SD height.
items.reference: Case 3: "Of the endocrine causes, the only abnormality resulting in this degree of SD in height is X-linked acrogigantism (Answer A)"

items.assertion: Assertion: IGSF1 deficiency can elevate both GH and IGF-1 without clinical acromegaly.
items.reason: Reason: IGSF1 deficiency always presents with pituitary gigantism.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—no acromegaly/gigantism phenotype.
items.reference: Biochemical Alterations: "both high GH and IGF-1 can be seen without the patient having clinical acromegaly: the recently described IGSF1 deficiency syndrome."

items.assertion: Assertion: Severe insulin resistance can produce acromegaloid clinical features.
items.reason: Reason: Hyperinsulinemia is among the most common pseudoacromegaly presentations in endocrine clinics.
items.explanation: Assertion true; hyperinsulinemia is rare except as noted—reason overstates frequency.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Apart from hyperinsulinemia, pseudoacromegaly states are rare conditions"
